[JS Weird World] No.4 The Execution Context: Creation and Hoisting
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

This chapter digs into one of the genuinely strange corners of JavaScript: "creation and hoisting." It's a topic that trips up a ton of people. We'll walk through what actually happens when JavaScript runs your code, why a variable can come back as undefined before you ever assigned it, and what hoisting really means (hint: your code doesn't actually move).
[JS Weird World] No.3 The Global Environment and the Global Object
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

The "global environment" is tightly tied to the idea of an execution context. When your program first runs, it runs inside the global environment — a prebuilt wrapper someone else wrote, a "Global" that holds both the code you're about to run and the code that's already running. This post walks through the global object, the special `this` variable, and where your variables and functions actually live.
[JS Weird World] No.2 Quick Concept Check: Name/Value Pairs and Objects
JavaScript Deep Dive: Conquering the Weird Parts

A name only ever pairs with one single value — same idea as a variable only holding one value at a time. A name can be defined and reassigned many times, but at any given moment it maps to just one value. This post also covers how objects in JavaScript are really just collections of name/value pairs.

People throw around the word "closure" all the time in JavaScript, but what does it actually mean? In short, it's a function declared inside another function. This post walks through closures with a simple example, shows how variable scope pollution causes bugs, and explains how a closure fixes it.
